meta.title: Forcing Function Architecture
meta.tl;dr: Don't add checklists. Design layouts where the right action is the easy action.

problem.statement: Agents with explicit checklists are fragile. They skip steps under load.
problem.example: A captain might forget to check bilge if it's a separate task. But if the dipstick is on the path to the hydraulic switch, checking becomes automatic.
problem.root_cause: Procedural safety (checklists) competes with operational pressure. Architectural safety (layout) has no competition.

principle.name: Forcing Function
principle.definition: A system architecture where the correct behavior is the path of least resistance

marine_proof.mechanism: Dipstick placed on walkway between bridge and hydraulic selector
marine_proof.natural_timing: Engine at idle-not-in-gear exactly when switching hydraulics before departure
marine_proof.result: Oil check happens every departure without being a task

application_to_agents.pattern: Digital twin room layout mirrors physical vessel paths

implementation.do: Make bilge_sensor a required field on the path from bridge room to hydraulics room in the twin
implementation.why: Agent naturally reads bilge when 'walking' to hydraulics. No task, no skip, no failure mode.

generalization.rule: Every safety-critical read should be architecturally required, not procedurally requested
generalization.test: Remove all checklists. If the agent still does the right thing, architecture is correct.

implications.for_cocapn: Room graph in digital twin IS the safety system. Not separate from it.
implications.for_fleet: Agent repos should structure their code so that hot-path functions naturally validate inputs from cold-path sensors.
implications.for_design: Every 'remember to check X' is a design failure. Redesign until checking X is unavoidable.

contradictions: Checklists are still needed for rare events not on any natural path
